articles.headline: Proceedings of the Meeting of Colored Citizens, New Bedford, Mass.
articles.summary: At a meeting of the Colored Citizens of New Bedford held in the Colored Christian Church, Richard C. Johnson was called to the chair and John Bowen (OCR: "Bowes") chosen Secretary. A committee of five (Ezra R. Johnson, William P. Powell, and Nathan Johnson named among them) was appointed to nominate candidates for the General Court; resolutions declared the meeting's abolitionist principles superior to party politics and pledged to vote only for candidates favorable to impartial liberty. Meeting adjourned to Friday, Nov. 10th.

articles.headline: Declaration of Sentiments, Methodist Anti-Slavery Convention, Lynn, Mass.
articles.summary: Full committee report and declaration adopted by the Methodist Anti-Slavery Convention at Lynn, MA, Oct. 26, 1837, condemning slavery as sin, calling for immediate emancipation without expatriation, denouncing ministers who apologize for slavery, and urging kind treatment of colored people in the North. Signed by the committee: Orange Scott, Schuyler Chamberlaine, Jonathan Hazleton, Guy Beckley, and Thomas H. Atwill.

articles.headline: Marriages and deaths, November 1837
articles.summary: Married: Nov. 9, by Rev. J. Golden, Mr. H. C. Turner to Miss Rachel Sanders of Carlisle; Oct. 30, by Rev. Dr. Weeks (Newark), Mr. Uor Stevenson of Williamstown, Mass. to Miss Violetta B. Swain of Trenton, NJ; Nov. 2, by Rev. P. Williams, Mr. Wm. B. Wilson to Miss Mary Anne Garret Marshall, both of New York. Died: Oct. 11, Mrs. Nancy Spencer, aged 34, Shrewsbury, NJ; Nov. 4, James C. Anderson, infant son of Mr. Charles Anderson, aged 6 months 7 days.

articles.headline: An American Citizen murdered!! The Press destroyed!!! -- editorial on the killing of Elijah P. Lovejoy
articles.summary: Lead editorial denouncing the Nov. 7, 1837 mob murder of Rev. Elijah P. Lovejoy at Alton, Illinois while defending his anti-slavery press, with a detailed account of the mob's repeated destruction of Lovejoy's presses, the final night attack, and Lovejoy's death defending the building; accompanied by a memorial poem 'Occasioned by the death of the Rev. Elijah P. Lovejoy, at Alton, Ill.' signed R.S.

articles.headline: Rhode Island State Anti-Slavery Society meeting -- Mr. Remond's address on employment for young colored men
articles.summary: At the Rhode Island State Anti-Slavery Society meeting, a resolution was introduced on the duty of abolitionists to help young men of color obtain clerkships and situations suited to their talents; Mr. Remond, a young 'Colored American' of Newport, delivered an address that made a favorable impression and was requested to write it out for publication.

articles.headline: Editorial correspondence from Philadelphia -- addressed to Jennings, Wake, and Barbary
articles.author: The Editor
articles.summary: Letter dated Philadelphia, Nov. 20, 1837, addressed to Thomas L. Jinnings [Jennings], Ransom F. Wage [Wake], and Zachariah Barbary describing travel from New York, treatment of colored passengers aboard the steamboat, and observations on Philadelphia church life; a companion piece ('Religion in New-York,' signed R.S.) criticizes prejudice and 'negro pews' in Northern white churches, including at Mr. Barnes's Presbyterian church on Washington Square, describing a colored Sabbath-school girl seated apart from white scholars.

articles.headline: Right of Suffrage petition committee enlarged
articles.summary: The committee appointed by an August public meeting of colored citizens of the State to prepare suffrage-extension petitions to the Legislature increased its membership; new names given (partly garbled in the source): Thomas L. Jennings (Chairman), Ransom F. Wake, Zachariah S. Barbary, Eli Hazzard, Charles B. Ray, and others. Thomas S. Sidney, Secretary of the Committee, notes an adjourned meeting at 69 Leonard Street.

articles.headline: Marriage, November 1837
articles.summary: Married in New York on Monday evening, Nov. 21, by Rev. Mr. Richardson, Mr. James Davis to Miss Clementena Young, both of Washington City.

articles.headline: Julius C. Morel's letter praising Stephen H. Gloucester
articles.author: Julius C. Morel
articles.summary: Editor's note introducing an extract from a letter of Julius C. Morel to a Philadelphia friend, praising Stephen H. Gloucester -- newly announced General Agent of the Colored American for Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland, and West Jersey (office on Lombard Street, Philadelphia) -- as a man who would devote his 'whole life and giant intellect' to the cause of the downtrodden colored man and the slave were he not obliged to earn a living.

articles.headline: Extracts from Dr. [James McCune] Smith's Journal -- transatlantic crossing anecdote of John, a young African sailor
articles.author: James McCune Smith
articles.summary: Serialized extracts from Dr. Smith's Atlantic crossing journal. Recounts an incident aboard ship: John, 'a boy of nineteen' whom an Englishman had brought from the coast of Africa, was pulled down from the cathead by an Irish passenger who said he had no right there 'because he was black'; John answered with a blow that drew blood and a fight ensued until the mate parted them, after which John was 'dubbed the belle of the ship.' Also recounts Smith's shipboard reflections on American vs. British naval architecture and a near-collision with an Irish fishing boat.

articles.headline: New York meeting on the murder of Rev. Elijah P. Lovejoy
articles.summary: At a meeting of colored citizens held in the First Colored Presbyterian Church, New York, on Thursday, Nov. 23, 1837, called in view of Lovejoy's murder, Rev. Theodore S. Wright occupied the chair and Rev. Charles B. Ray acted as secretary. Resolutions (adopted Nov. 29, 1837) mourned Lovejoy as the first martyr in the cause of abolition, expressed sympathy for his widow and children, pledged renewed commitment to abolition principles, and recommended similar meetings elsewhere.

articles.headline: Editorial correspondence from Philadelphia -- the Philadelphia Library for Colored People and Sarah Douglass's school
articles.author: The Editor
articles.summary: Second installment of editorial travel letters from Philadelphia (dated Nov. 20, 1837 and Nov. 24, 1837). Describes a visit to the Philadelphia Library for Colored People, founded by Mr. [Frederick A.] Hinton and other 'patriotic brethren' some two or three years earlier, incorporated, with nearly 1,000 volumes. Also describes an extended visit to Miss Sarah [Mapps] Douglass's school for over 40 pupils, praising her cabinet of shells and minerals and her instruction in natural science alongside standard female education.

articles.headline: Public meeting at the Fifth Presbyterian Church, Philadelphia, in support of the Colored American
articles.summary: At the Concert of Prayer for the abolition of slavery at the Fifth Presbyterian Church, St. Mary Street, Philadelphia, Rev. Samuel E. Cornish addressed the meeting on the utility of a concerted press. Afterward the house organized under Rev. Wm. Douglass (Chairman) and J. C. White (Secretary); F. A. Hinton offered resolutions, seconded and adopted, recognizing the Colored American as an important auxiliary of emancipation and appointing a ten-member committee to extend its circulation; a resolution on the paper's name was moved by Robert C. Gordon and seconded by J. C. White.

articles.headline: Fugitive-slave arrest of Alfred Canada
articles.summary: Alfred Canada was arrested in New York and brought before the Recorder, claimed as a fugitive slave from North Carolina by a man calling himself Canada. The correspondent (signed 'P.W.') criticizes the Recorder for failing to honor an earlier promise to notify designated individuals before any such trial proceeded, and reports Alfred was shipped south aboard the schooner New York.

articles.headline: Deaths, late November 1837
articles.summary: Died: Friday, Nov. 24, Mr. Theodore Montonio, aged 51. Nov. 5, Mr. John Valentine, aged 60, a longtime member of Zion's Methodist Church in New York, described as a man of profound piety and integrity.

articles.headline: A Voice from Wales -- Welsh anti-slavery memorial to Welsh Americans
articles.summary: Memorial adopted by Welsh county assemblies (Merionethshire, Carnarvonshire, and Montgomeryshire, May-June 1837) addressed to Welsh-descended Americans, urging them to use all lawful means to end American slavery; signed by Edward Davis, William Jones, and Wm. Williamson, 'and above thirty others.'

articles.headline: Philadelphia Committee -- full roster to cooperate with the New York publication committee
articles.summary: Names the Philadelphia committee organized to operate jointly with the New York committee publishing the Colored American: Rev. Wm. Douglass, Chairman; Stephen H. Gloucester, Agent; Members F. A. Hinton, John Bowers Jr., S. H. Gloucester, Thomas Black, Robert C. Gordon Jr., James Newman, Jas. C. Bias, James Needham, and Thomas Butler.

articles.headline: Reorganization of the Roger Williams Anti-Slavery Society as the Zion Baptist Anti-Slavery Society
articles.summary: Notice of the dissolution and remodeling of the Roger Williams Anti-Slavery Society, reformed as the Zion Baptist Anti-Slavery Society. Officers: Elder J. T. Raymond, President; Mr. J. Merryweather, Vice President; Mr. W. Hall, Secretary; Deacon L. Octon, Treasurer. Managers: S. Angell, A. Miller, J. T. Smith, J. Kinney (also rendered J. Kinnes), B. Bowen, W. Henry, H. Winston, W. Thompson, J. Smith, I. Pierce, J. Gardner, C. Smith, D. Copeland, and W. Hodges.

articles.headline: Public discussion notice -- Non-Resistance debate at the Broadway Tabernacle
articles.summary: Notice of a public discussion on Non-Resistance at the Broadway Tabernacle, proceeds to defray suffrage-petition expenses. Affirmative side to be argued by Dr. John Brown, P. A. Bell, and David Ruggles; negative side by H. H. Garnet(t), W. J. Wilson, and W. P. Johnson. Committee: W. A. Tyson and W. P. Johnson.

articles.headline: First Anniversary of the United Daughters of Conference, Zion's Church
articles.author: An Observer
articles.summary: The First Anniversary of the United Daughters of Conference was held at Zion's Church, Nov. 22, 1837. An address was delivered by Rev. J. Richardson, pastor of the church, followed by remarks from Rev. Christopher Rush, Superintendent of the Zion's Connection of Methodists. The Society raises funds to support the ministry of the Zion Methodist Episcopal Church.

articles.headline: J. W. Duffin's letter on prejudice witnessed at a Geneva, NY debate
articles.author: J. W. Duffin
articles.summary: Duffin writes from Geneva, NY (Nov. 8, 1837) describing attending a public debate of the Young Men's Association in a Presbyterian church lecture room, where he and a companion were repeatedly shunned by white attendees who moved away upon seeing their faces, provoking laughter from onlookers; he reflects on the sinfulness of such prejudice within the church.

articles.headline: Aaron L. Poyer commended for Sabbath observance
articles.author: S. A.
articles.summary: Short item praising Mr. Aaron L. Poyer, hair dresser at No. 45 West Broadway, for keeping his shop closed on the Sabbath, urging other 'Colored Americans' to follow his example.

articles.headline: Public meeting protesting Judge Betts's decision in the Henry Metscher case
articles.summary: At a public meeting of aggrieved colored citizens held at Asbury Church, Nov. 7, 1837, protesting Judge Betts's decision consigning Henry Metscher to slavery without regard to his proof of freedom, Rev. Edward Crosby was called to the chair and Edward V. Clark appointed Secretary. David Ruggles, chairman of the resolutions committee, reported ten resolutions condemning the decision and the 'kidnapping club' operating in the city; a clause declining to recommend non-resistance sparked debate, opposed by Revs. Theodore S. Wright, Jacob Matthews, and Charles B. Ray and by Mr. W. P. Johnson, defended at length by Ruggles, and ultimately rejected by vote.

events.name: Meeting of Colored Citizens of New Bedford, Massachusetts
events.description: Public meeting of colored citizens of New Bedford in the Colored Christian Church to nominate candidates for the General Court and adopt abolitionist voting resolutions.

events.name: New York meeting mourning the murder of Rev. Elijah P. Lovejoy
events.location: First Colored Presbyterian Church, New York City
events.description: Meeting of colored citizens called in view of the murder of Elijah P. Lovejoy at Alton, Illinois; resolutions of mourning, sympathy for his widow, and renewed anti-slavery commitment were adopted (dated Nov. 29, 1837).

events.name: Concert of Prayer meeting, Fifth Presbyterian Church, Philadelphia, in support of the Colored American
events.location: Fifth Presbyterian Church, St. Mary Street, Philadelphia
events.description: Meeting following a Concert of Prayer for the abolition of slavery; Rev. Samuel E. Cornish addressed the assembly on the value of the press, and the house organized to support the Colored American, adopting resolutions offered by F. A. Hinton.

events.name: Philadelphia Committee to cooperate with New York in publishing the Colored American
events.description: Roster of the Philadelphia committee organized to jointly maintain the Colored American newspaper alongside the New York committee.

events.name: Zion Baptist Anti-Slavery Society -- reorganization from the Roger Williams Anti-Slavery Society
events.description: Dissolution of the Roger Williams Anti-Slavery Society and reformation as the Zion Baptist Anti-Slavery Society, with new officers and a board of managers.

events.name: Public meeting protesting Judge Betts's decision in the Henry Metscher fugitive-slave case
events.description: Public meeting of aggrieved colored citizens protesting Judge Betts's ruling against Henry Metscher's freedom claim; adopted resolutions condemning the decision and the city's 'kidnapping club,' with a contested debate over a non-resistance clause.
